blocks.content:
Course Instructor: Romana Maroof
Email: Romana.maroof@au.edu.pk
Course: Computer Organization & Assembly Language

items: Understand the purpose of flags in the CPU
Learn about different types of flags in 8086
Demonstrate effect of instructions on flags
Write and execute programs that manipulate flags

blocks.content: Flags are special bits in the Flag Register that are set (1) or cleared (0) after operations. They indicate processor status or result of an operation and influence control flow in programs.

items: Status Flags → Indicate results of arithmetic/logic instructions
System Flags → Manage operating system–level operations

blocks.content: The EFLAGS register contains status flags (status of arithmetic and logical operations) and control/system flags (control CPU operation). Programs can set and clear individual bits in the EFLAGS register.

items: Status Flags: Zero Flag (ZF), Sign Flag (SF), Carry Flag (CF), Overflow Flag (OF), Parity Flag (PF)
Control Flags: Direction Flag (DF), Interrupt Flag (IF), Trap Flag (TF)

blocks.definition: Set when unsigned arithmetic result is out of range. Set when an unsigned arithmetic operation generates a carry out of the MSB (most significant bit) or a borrow in subtraction.
Set when signed arithmetic result is out of range. Set when a signed arithmetic operation results in a value too large/small to fit in destination register.
Copy of sign bit. Set if the result is negative (MSB = 1 in signed interpretation).
Set if there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (used in BCD arithmetic).
Sums the number of bits that are set in a number, and indicates whether the sum is odd or even. Set when parity is even.

blocks.content:
; Carry Flag Example
MOV AL, 0FFH   ; 255 in AL
ADD AL, 01H    ; Result = 0, Carry = 1
; CF = 1 because 255+1 = 256, out of 8-bit range

; Overflow Flag Example
MOV AL, 7FH    ; 127 (max positive signed 8-bit)
ADD AL, 01H    ; Result = -128, OF = 1
; Because signed range is -128 to +127

; Sign Flag Example
MOV AL, 05H
SUB AL, 07H    ; Result = -2 (FEH in hex)
; SF = 1 because result is negative

; Zero Flag Example
MOV AL, 02H
SUB AL, 02H    ; Result = 0
; ZF = 1 because subtraction gave zero

; Auxiliary Carry Example
MOV AL, 09H
ADD AL, 01H    ; Result = 0AH, carry from bit 3 to 4
; AF = 1

; Parity Flag Example
MOV AL, 04H    ; 00000100 (only 1 one → odd parity)
; PF = 0
XOR AL, AL     ; Result = 0 (00000000, 0 ones → even parity)
; PF = 1

blocks.definition: Controls the direction in which string instructions (like MOVS, LODS, STOS, SCAS, CMPS) process data in memory. When DF = 0: String operations process forward (from lower to higher memory addresses). When DF = 1: String operations process backward (from higher to lower memory addresses).
Controls whether the CPU will respond to maskable hardware interrupts. When IF = 1: CPU responds to external interrupts. When IF = 0: CPU ignores external interrupts.
